surviving.axisLabel: transaction state + watch + RYW locking races
surviving.title: Watch goroutine vs Cancel/Reset race on tx.watchCtx/watchCancel: data race + lost-cancellation goroutine leak
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1863 (getWatchCtx — unlocked read/write of watchCtx,watchCancel) and transaction.go:1852 (cancelWatches — unlocked read/write of the same fields); the concurrent caller is readpath.go:1117 (WatchPoll calls getWatchCtx) running in the goroutine spawned at fdb/future.go:177 via fdb/transaction.go:443-448. The lost-cancellation window exists because WatchSetup (readpath.go:1069-1103) never sets watchCtx/watchCancel.
surviving.cppReference: n/a — Go-intrinsic data race + goroutine/handle leak; no libfdb_c divergence is involved (finder's cppReference n/a is correct).
surviving.description: tx.watchCtx and tx.watchCancel are plain fields accessed with NO mutex/atomic. getWatchCtx (transaction.go:1864-1867) reads watchCtx==nil and assigns both fields; it is called from WatchPoll (readpath.go:1117), which runs in the async watch-future goroutine spawned by newFutureNil in fdb/transaction.go:443 (the fdb facade Watch returns a FutureNil whose body calls inner.WatchPoll). cancelWatches (transaction.go:1853-1856) reads watchCancel and nils both fields; it is called from Cancel() (transaction.go:1723) and reset() (transaction.go:2659, the OnError-retry and user Reset() path). The watch future goroutine runs concurrently with the caller continuing to use the transaction (this concurrency is explicitly by design — see the comments at transaction.go:161, :2613, :2669 acknowledging 'Watch() goroutines may still be running'). Two harms: (1) a plain data race — concurrent unlocked read+write of the same fields, flagged by `go test -race` and UB under the Go memory model; (2) a lost-cancellation goroutine+context leak: WatchSetup (synchronous) does NOT touch watchCtx, so after Watch() returns watchCtx is still nil; if cancelWatches() reads watchCancel==nil (before the watch goroutine has reached getWatchCtx) it becomes a no-op, then the watch goroutine mints a fresh context.WithCancel that will NEVER be cancelled (Cancel/Reset are one-shot and already ran) and enters the unbounded long-poll loop (sendWatch in readpath.go:1251 blocks on the reply channel with no timeout). The goroutine and its storage-server reply handle leak until the user's parent ctx is cancelled or the watched key changes — defeating the documented contract that Cancel()/Reset() cancel in-flight watches (transaction.go:1734-1736).
surviving.whyWrong: Go-intrinsic concurrency bug; no C++ spec needed. The two functions mutate shared pointer fields with no synchronization while the codebase's own comments establish the watch goroutine runs concurrently with Cancel/Reset.
surviving.repro:
Two deterministic repros, both intrinsic (no C++ comparison):
(A) Data race, run with -race:
  tx := newTestTx()
  var wg sync.WaitGroup; wg.Add(2)
  go func(){ defer wg.Done(); tx.getWatchCtx(context.Background()) }()
  go func(){ defer wg.Done(); tx.Cancel() }()  // Cancel -> cancelWatches
  wg.Wait()
The race detector reports concurrent unsynchronized read/write of tx.watchCtx / tx.watchCancel (getWatchCtx at transaction.go:1864-1865 vs cancelWatches at transaction.go:1854-1856).

(B) Lost-cancellation, single-threaded deterministic demonstration of the losing order:
  tx := newTestTx()
  tx.Cancel()                                    // cancelWatches: watchCancel==nil -> no-op
  wctx := tx.getWatchCtx(context.Background())    // mints a FRESH live context
  if wctx.Err() != nil { t.Fatal("bug: expected uncancelled ctx after Cancel") } // currently passes
This shows a watch armed in the cancelWatches-before-getWatchCtx order gets a context that Cancel/Reset will never cancel — the in-flight watch leaks (the WatchPoll loop at readpath.go:1132-1166 only watches watchCtx.Err(), never tx.state). Correct behavior: getWatchCtx after Cancel/Reset must return an already-cancelled context (or WatchPoll must observe the cancelled tx state) so the watch goroutine drains.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
Verified entirely from the Go source (this is a Go-intrinsic concurrency bug; no C++ spec applies, so cppReference n/a is correct).

FIELDS UNPROTECTED: transaction.go:400-401 declares `watchCtx context.Context` and `watchCancel context.CancelFunc` as plain struct fields with no mutex/atomic.

getWatchCtx (transaction.go:1863-1868): `if tx.watchCtx == nil { tx.watchCtx, tx.watchCancel = context.WithCancel(parent) }` — unlocked read of watchCtx, unlocked write of both fields.

cancelWatches (transaction.go:1852-1858): `if tx.watchCancel != nil { tx.watchCancel(); tx.watchCtx = nil; tx.watchCancel = nil }` — unlocked read of watchCancel, unlocked write of both fields. Called from Cancel() (transaction.go:1723) and reset() (transaction.go:2659; reset() is invoked by user Reset() at :1749 and by the OnError retry path).

CONCURRENCY IS REAL AND BY DESIGN: getWatchCtx is reached only from WatchPoll (readpath.go:1117, `watchCtx := tx.getWatchCtx(ctx)`, no surrounding lock). WatchPoll runs in the goroutine spawned by newFutureNil (fdb/future.go:177 `go func(){...}`) via the facade Watch (fdb/transaction.go:443-448 returns newFutureNil whose body calls inner.WatchPoll). The codebase's own comments confirm this goroutine runs concurrently with reset/cancel: transaction.go:2669-2671 ("Watch() goroutines may still be running after cancelWatches()"). So whenever a Watch goroutine is live and the caller (or the OnError retry path) calls Cancel()/Reset(), getWatchCtx and cancelWatches touch the same two pointer fields with zero synchronization => a data race under the Go memory model, flagged by `go test -race`.

LOST-CANCELLATION LEAK: WatchSetup (readpath.go:1069-1103) is the synchronous half and never touches watchCtx/watchCancel — confirmed by reading the full function; it only pins readVersion, captures span, adds the read conflict, and reads the value. So after Watch() returns, tx.watchCtx==nil and tx.watchCancel==nil until the async WatchPoll first calls getWatchCtx. If cancelWatches() runs in that window it observes watchCancel==nil and is a no-op; the goroutine then reaches getWatchCtx, mints a fresh context.WithCancel(parent) whose Err()==nil, and enters WatchPoll's loop (readpath.go:1132-1166) which checks only watchCtx.Err() (never tx.state) and blocks in sendWatch's unbounded long-poll (readpath.go:1251 select on replyCh / ctx.Done with no timeout). The just-issued Cancel()/Reset() — being one-shot — never cancels this fresh context, so the watch goroutine and its storage-server reply handle leak until the parent ctx (tr.t.ctx, possibly long-lived/Background-rooted) fires or the key changes. This violates the documented contract at transaction.go:1734-1736 that in-flight Watch() calls ARE cancelled by Reset()/Cancel().

NOT ALREADY TESTED: TestCancel_CancelsActiveWatchContext (transaction_unit_test.go:622-638) is single-threaded and calls getWatchCtx (line 626) strictly before Cancel() (line 630); TestGetWatchCtx_LazyAndIdempotent (640-651) is also single-threaded. Neither exercises the concurrent unlocked access nor the cancel-before-getWatchCtx order. No -race concurrent regression exists for this path.

surviving.title: AddReadConflictRange / AddReadConflictKey skip the RYW write-map filter (updateConflictMap), over-conflicting when a local independent Set/Clear precedes the explicit conflict-range add
surviving.description: Go adds the full explicit read-conflict range/key to tx.readConflicts regardless of the transaction's pending local writes; libfdb_c filters that range through the write map and drops segments already satisfied by an independent local Set or a local Clear. Result: a commit that libfdb_c accepts, Go rejects with not_committed (1020), when an in-range key was locally Set/Cleared BEFORE the AddReadConflictRange/Key call.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ ReadYourWritesTransaction::addReadConflictRange (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1945), in the RYW-enabled branch, does NOT add the full user range to readConflicts: it builds a WriteMap::iterator, skips to begin, and calls updateConflictMap(readRange, it) (lines 1983-1986). updateConflictMap(KeyRangeRef) (lines 335-351) inserts a sub-range into readConflicts ONLY when `it.is_unmodified_range() || (it.is_operation() && !it.is_independent())` (line 342). A plain SetValue is independent (WriteMap.cpp:99-100 sets is_dependent=false for SetValue; WriteMap.h:171-173 is_independent() returns true) and a cleared range is neither is_unmodified_range nor is_operation, so both are SUBTRACTED — that segment gets no read conflict. The full-range add only happens on the readYourWritesDisabled path (lines 1977-1980). Go's AddReadConflictRange (transaction.go:2553) calls tx.addReadConflict(begin,end) at line 2565 unconditionally, and AddReadConflictKey (line 2570) calls addReadConflictForKey directly — neither consults tx.ryw's write map, so Go adds read conflicts that libfdb_c filters out. Note the Go conflictRangesLocked/conflictForKeyLocked filter machinery already exists and is correctly applied to GetKey/GetRange (RFC-058/RFC-121), but was never wired into the explicit AddReadConflictRange/Key path.
surviving.repro: Two txns A,B on the same DB pinned to the same read version v0. A: Set(K,"A"); AddReadConflictRange(K, keyAfter(K))  (Set FIRST — the order that triggers the filter; AddReadConflictKey(K) behaves identically). B: Set(K,"B"); commit (lands at v1>v0). Then A.commit(). libfdb_c/cgo: A commits successfully — the read conflict on K is filtered out because A locally Set K with an independent write (its blind write wins). Go: A fails not_committed (1020) — Go kept the full [K, keyAfter(K)) read conflict, which conflicts with B's write at v1. Same divergence with A doing Clear(K) before AddReadConflictRange over [K, keyAfter(K)).

surviving.title: Transaction.Watch on a system/special/oversized key silently registers instead of returning key_outside_legal_range / key_too_large
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/readpath.go:1069 (WatchSetup) — also reached via readpath.go:1044 Transaction.Watch and fdb/transaction.go:442 facade Watch
surviving.description: C++ ReadYourWritesTransaction::watch validates the key BEFORE registering: `if (key >= allKeys.end || (key >= getMaxReadKey() && key != metadataVersionKey && tr.apiVersionAtLeast(300))) return key_outside_legal_range();` (RYW.actor.cpp:2451) and `if (key.size() > getMaxWriteKeySize(key, ...options.rawAccess)) return key_too_large();` (:2454). Go's WatchSetup performs NONE of these checks — after the rywDisabled gate it calls ensureReadVersion, AddReadConflictKey(key), then ryw.get(key). rywCache.get (ryw.go:370) has no legal-range check, and the only legal-range guard in the client lives in Transaction.Get (transaction.go:680), which the watch path does not call. So Go silently adds a read conflict on an illegal key and registers a storage-server watch where libfdb_c returns an error synchronously.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ watch() throws key_outside_legal_range (2004) for any key >= getMaxReadKey() (= \xff without READ_SYSTEM_KEYS, and always for >= \xff\xff = allKeys.end), and key_too_large (2102) for an oversized key, before any read-conflict is added or watch registered (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2451-2456). Go (readpath.go:1069 WatchSetup) instead proceeds: AddReadConflictKey on the illegal key (readpath.go:1090) and ryw.get → getValue, issuing a GetValue RPC and registering a watch. C++ returns an error; Go returns success.
surviving.repro: On a transaction WITHOUT SetReadSystemKeys/SetAccessSystemKeys: (a) tr.Watch([]byte("\xff/foo")) — libfdb_c returns key_outside_legal_range (2004); Go reads \xff/foo and registers a watch (no error). (b) tr.Watch([]byte("\xff\xff/status/json")) — libfdb_c 2004 (>= allKeys.end); Go proceeds. (c) tr.Watch(make([]byte, 10001)) (10001-byte normal key) — libfdb_c key_too_large (2102); Go proceeds. Differential: run the same Watch call against gofdb and cgofdb and compare the synchronous setup error.

surviving.title: db.wg.Add(1) in getReadVersion request path can race Close()'s wg.Wait() -> 'WaitGroup misuse: Add called concurrently with Wait' panic
surviving.description: getReadVersion lazily starts the background GRV refresher on the FIRST cache-opted-in request via `b.refreshOnce.Do(func(){ b.refresherStarted.Store(true); db.wg.Add(1); go b.backgroundRefresher(db) })` (grv.go:293-297). This db.wg.Add(1) executes on a user transaction's read path (Transaction.ensureReadVersion -> grvBatcher.getReadVersion, transaction.go:653). Database.Close() (database.go:974-984) does `db.cancel(); db.wg.Wait()`. The only baseline wg holder is topologyMonitor (added once at open, database.go:799), which exits and calls wg.Done() as soon as db.ctx is canceled. Go's sync.WaitGroup panics with 'sync: WaitGroup misuse: Add called concurrently with Wait' when Add(delta>0) observes a registered waiter AND the counter was exactly 0 (i.e. `w != 0 && delta>0 && v==delta`). That state is reachable here: Close's wg.Wait() parks while topologyMonitor is still alive (counter>0); topologyMonitor then sees ctx.Done and wg.Done()s, dropping the counter to 0; in that window a concurrent first opt-in getReadVersion runs db.wg.Add(1) -> the runtime aborts the whole process. The Add-in-request-path pattern is a textbook WaitGroup misuse; the refresher should be started without an Add on the consumption path (e.g. under closeOnce/lock, or gated on db.ctx, or tracked by a mechanism Close drains differently).
surviving.whyWrong: Go runtime contract: a positive WaitGroup.Add that starts when the counter is 0 must happen-before any Wait; calling Add concurrently with a parked Wait at counter==0 is defined misuse and panics. getReadVersion is part of the documented thread-safe API surface and can run concurrently with the (idempotent, thread-safe) Close(), so this Add-on-the-request-path violates that contract.
surviving.repro: Open a database whose DEFAULT-batcher refresher has never started (refreshOnce still pending — e.g. a process that has only issued non-opt-in transactions). Goroutine A: tx := db.CreateTransaction(); tx.SetUseGrvCache(); tx.ensureReadVersion(ctx)  // -> grvBatcher.getReadVersion -> refreshOnce.Do -> db.wg.Add(1). Goroutine B: db.Close()  // -> db.cancel(); db.wg.Wait(). Interleave so B's Wait parks while topologyMonitor is alive, topologyMonitor observes the cancel and wg.Done()s the counter to 0, and A's db.wg.Add(1) lands in the gap before Wait returns. Result: runtime panic 'sync: WaitGroup misuse: Add called concurrently with Wait' aborting the process (also flagged by -race). Gated behind USE_GRV_CACHE opt-in (default off) plus concurrent Close.

surviving.title: Hedge top-level ctx.Done() branch leaks the primary's QueueModel startRequest delta (permanent load-balancer skew)
surviving.description: In sendFrameWithHedge, after the primary frame is sent successfully (delta charged by startRequest inside the primary closure), the first select waits on pResult.replyCh, the hedge timer, and ctx.Done(). The `case <-ctx.Done()` branch returns `hedgeResult{err: ctx.Err()}` with NO addr, NO delta, and NO others. The caller's post-hedge accounting (readpath.go:514-525 / 320-330 / 886-896) ends deltas only via result.others and the `if result.addr != ""` block; with both empty/zero the primary's startRequest is NEVER matched by endRequest. queueData.smoothOutstanding.total is a permanent running sum (update() only decays `estimate` toward `total`, never shrinks `total`), so each occurrence permanently inflates servers[bestIdx]'s outstanding metric and biases chooseServer/chooseTopTwo away from that replica forever. This is exactly the RFC-010 #5 leak the surrounding code guards against: the two sibling ctx-cancel paths are correct (waitForReply at hedge.go:134 returns addr/delta/start; raceReplies at hedge.go:169 returns both arms in others) — only this top-level select branch drops the accounting. Affects all three read paths (sendGetValue, sendGetKey, sendGetRange) since they share the pattern.
surviving.whyWrong: The code's own invariant (RFC-010 #5, readpath.go:511-513: 'end every started request ... else its QueueModel delta leaks permanently and biases server selection') is violated only on this branch. waitForReply (hedge.go:133-137) and raceReplies (hedge.go:169-174) both preserve addr/delta or others on ctx.Done; sendFrameWithHedge's own select at line 99 does not — a self-inconsistency that is provably a leak.
surviving.repro: Hedge enabled (default; database.go:786 Store(true)) and a shard whose replica set has >=2 available servers so chooseTopTwo returns secondary != nil. Issue tx.Get/GetRange/GetKey over a WARM/pooled connection (getOrDial returns cached conn, SendFrame succeeds so pResult.err==nil and we enter the select at hedge.go:82). Cancel the caller ctx (or let a tx SetTimeout opContext deadline elapse) after the primary frame is sent but before the primary reply arrives and before the hedgeDelay (~max(10ms, 2*latency)) timer fires. sendFrameWithHedge returns hedgeResult{err: ctx.Err()} with addr="" and others=nil; the caller skips all endRequest calls; queueModel.getOrCreate(servers[bestIdx].Address).smoothOutstanding.total stays inflated by the leaked delta (penalty, default 1.0). Unit repro: drive sendFrameWithHedge with an already-cancelled ctx, a non-nil secondary, and a primary whose replyCh never fires (like TestHedge_ContextCancellation but with secondary != nil), then assert totalOf(qm, primaryAddr) returns to baseline after applyCallerAccounting — it does not. Existing TestHedge_ContextCancellation passes nil secondary, so it routes through waitForReply and never exercises this branch.

surviving.title: sendGetValue sequential fallback swallows genuine FDB reply errors, masking them as all_alternatives_failed / transaction_too_old
surviving.description: After the hedge result carries an error (connErr or errReplyTimeout), sendGetValue falls back to scanning the remaining servers (readpath.go:535-558). The loop returns on success, sets sawTimeout on isReplyTimeout, and returns on isWrongShardServer/isAllAlternativesFailed — but any OTHER FDB error from sendGetValueToServer (e.g. future_version 1009, process_behind 1037, transaction_too_old 1007, or a tenant/permission error parsed from the reply) falls through with no return and no record. After the loop it returns errReplyTimeout (if any timeout was seen) or all_alternatives_failed, which getValueImpl then turns into a retryable transaction_too_old. In the normal single-arm path (readpath.go:561-563) that same error is returned directly to getValueImpl and bubbled up. So the two paths diverge: a genuine reply error encountered only on the post-hedge fallback is lost. For retryable errors the app-observable outcome is still retryable (benign); a genuinely non-retryable reply error would be converted into an effectively-infinite Transact retry.
surviving.whyWrong: Behavior is inconsistent between the happy single-arm path (returns the real error) and the fallback path (drops it). To prove a hard divergence from libfdb_c one must confirm what loadBalance/getValue (NativeAPI.actor.cpp) does when an alternative returns future_version/process_behind vs a terminal reply error; the Go fallback unconditionally collapses all of them to all_alternatives_failed.
surviving.repro: Shard with >=2 servers; make the hedge primary fail with a connection error or reply timeout (so result.err != nil and the fallback loop runs), and make a fallback server's GetValueReply carry a non-wrong-shard FDB error (e.g. 1009 future_version or a terminal code). Observe that getValue returns transaction_too_old/all_alternatives_failed instead of the actual reply error the single-arm path would surface. Confirm the C++ disposition of the same per-alternative error to grade as divergence.
